pages.text: Water absorption, permeability, and resistance to chloride-ion penetration of lightweight aggregate concrete Xuemei Liu *, Kok Seng Chia, Min-Hong Zhang Department of Civil Engineering, National University of Singapore, E1A 07-03, 1 Engineering Drive 2, 117576, Singapore article info Article history: Received 24 June 2009 Received in revised form 3 May 2010 Accepted 7 June 2010 Available online 8 July 2010 Keywords: Cement paste Concrete Diffusivity Internal curing Interface transition zone Lightweight aggregate Microstructure Migration Permeability Sorptivity abstract This paper presents an experimental study to evaluate effect of cumulative lightweight aggregate (LWA) content (including lightweight sand) in concrete [water/cement ratio ( w/c) = 0.38] on its water absorp- tion, water permeability, and resistance to chloride-ion penetration. Rapid chloride penetrability test (ASTM C 1202), rapid migration test (NT Build 492), and salt ponding test (AASHTO T 259) were con- ducted to evaluate the concrete resistance to chloride-ion penetration. The results were compared with those of a cement paste and a control normal weight aggregate concrete (NWAC) with the same w/c and a NWAC (w/c = 0.54) with 28-day compressive strength similar to some of the lightweight aggregate con- crete (LWAC). Results indicate that although the total charge passed, migration coefﬁcient, and diffusion coefﬁcient of the LWAC were not signiﬁcantly different from those of NWAC with the same w/c of 0.38, resistance of the LWAC to chloride penetration decreased with increase in the cumulative LWA content in the concretes. The water penetration depth under pressure and water sorptivity showed, in general, sim- ilar trends. The LWAC with only coarse LWA had similar water sorptivity, water permeability coefﬁcient, and resistance to chloride-ion penetration compared to NWAC with similar w/c. The LWAC had lower water sorptivity, water permeability and higher resistance to chloride-ion penetration than the NWAC with similar 28-day strength but higher w/c. Both the NWAC and LWAC had lower sorptivity and higher resistance to chloride-ion penetration than the cement paste with similar w/c. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Lightweight aggregate concrete (LWAC) has been used for struc- tural applications in long-span bridges, ﬂoating structures, high- rise buildings, and buildings where soil conditions are poor for many years. In some LWAC, only coarse lightweight aggregates (LWA) are used, whereas in others, both coarse and ﬁne LWA are used. For ﬂoating structures, both coarse and ﬁne LWA should be considered to reduce the self weight of structures for economical use of the LWAC. When such structure is exposed to aggressive environments such as marine environments, the long-term dura- bility of the structure is to a large extent controlled by the perme- ability and resistance to chloride-ion penetration of concrete. In LWAC used for applications in severe environments, LWA is generally more porous than cement paste matrices. For concrete with only coarse LWA, if the porous aggregate particles are evenly distributed and embedded in dense mortar matrix (assume natural sand particles have negligible porosity), harmful substances may not be able to penetrate into the concrete easily. With the intro- duction and increase in porous ﬁne LWA particles in concrete in addition to the coarse LWA particles, however, the porosity of the mortar is increased ( Fig

the electrical conductivity and chloride penetrability (determined by ASTM C 1202 and bulk diffusion test) of LWAC containing both coarse and ﬁne LWA with w/c of 0.30 and made with blended ce- ment with silica fume were substantially lower than those of NWAC. Most LWAC used for long-span bridges and ﬂoating structures built in the last 30 years incorporated only coarse LWA with parti- cle size greater than 4 mm and ﬁne natural sands. For example, the LWAC used for Heidrun tension leg platform in the North Sea and the Nordhordland ﬂoating bridge in Norway mentioned in Ref. [5] used natural sand and coarse LWA with concrete density of more than 1800 kg/m 3. In order to take full advantage of lower unit weight LWAC with both coarse and ﬁne LWA and to use such concrete potentially in ﬂoating structures in marine environment, the permeability and resistance to chloride-ion penetration of such concretes need to be evaluated systematically. This paper presents an experimental study to evaluate the effect of cumulative LWA content (including lightweight sand) in concrete [ w/c = 0.38] on its water permeabil- ity, water absorption, and resistance to chloride-ion penetration. Standard tests like rapid chloride penetrability test (ASTM C 1202 [6]), rapid migration test (NT Build 492 [7]), and salt ponding test (AASHTO T 259 [8]) were conducted to evaluate the concrete resistance to chloride-ion penetration. The results were compared with those of a cement paste and a control NWAC with the same w/ c and a NWAC ( w/c = 0.54) with 28-day compressive strength sim- ilar to some of the LWAC and discussed. 2. Experimental details 2.1. Materials and specimen preparation ASTM Type I normal Portland cement was used. A dark brown solution of naph- thalene based superplasticizer 1 which contained 40% solids and had a speciﬁc grav- ity of 1.2 was used. Granite aggregate with a maximum size of 9.5 mm was used for the control concretes NC1 and NC2. Natural sand was used in all the concretes except LC4. Den- sities of the granite and natural sand were 2610 and 2560 kg/m 3, respectively. The aggregates were separated into four fractions of <1.18 mm, 1.18–2.36 mm, 2.36– 4.75 mm, and 4.75–9.5 mm, and then recombined to satisfy ASTM C 33 [9] grading requirements. Lightweight aggregates used in Mixtures LC1 to LC4 were commercially avail- able expanded clay 2 with four size fractions corresponding to those of the normal weight aggregates (NWA) aforementioned. Properties of the LWA are summarized in Table 1 . The LWA with sizes >1.18 mm had a spherical shape and water absorp- tion of 8.4–10.8% at 1 h, and 11.6–13.0% at 24 h. The size <1.18 mm consisted of crushed lightweight sand with more open pores and much higher water absorption of about 25–30% at both 1 h and 24 h. This is an estimated range as it is difﬁcult to determine absorption of the aggregate due to the nature of the crushed particles with higher open porosity and very small sizes. Total porosity of the LWA particles was calculated based on speciﬁc gravity of the aggregate particles and speciﬁc gravity of the solid material as follows: fa ¼ 1 /C0 qb qs ð1Þ where fa = total porosity of aggregate (%, by volume), qs = speciﬁc gravity of solid material and qb = speciﬁc gravity of aggregate particles. Speciﬁc gravity ( qs) of the solid material was determined by helium pycnometer using a ground LWA sample passing through a 150 lm sieve. Bulk speciﬁc gravity of the aggregate ( qb) was determined according to ASTM

used such that the cumulative volume of the LWA increased from 50% by volume of total aggregate in mixture LC1 to 100% in LC4. The LC4 had crushed lightweight sand with size <1.18 mm, and it was difﬁcult to determine the water absorption as mentioned earlier. Two concrete mixtures were prepared with the estimated absorptions of 25% and 30% for crushed lightweight sand, denoted as LC4-25 and LC4-30, respectively. The LC-30 had a slump of 85 mm without using superplasti- cizer (Table 2). When compared, NC1 and LC1–LC3 contained 2.3–2.7 L/m 3 superp- lasticizer with slumps from 70 to 125 mm. This indicated an overestimation of the water absorption in LC4-30. The LC4-25 which used 2.1 L/m 3 superplasticizer had a slump of 80 mm, similar to NC1 and LC1–LC3. Control NWAC with w/c of 0.38 (NC1) and 0.54 (NC2) were also included for comparison. The NC1 had the same w/c as that of the LWAC, whereas NC2 had sim- ilar 28-day strength to that of LC1 and LC2. In addition, a cement paste with a w/c of 0.38 was also included for comparison. For each mixture, specimens listed in Table 3 were cast. Density, compressive strength (adopted BS EN 12390: Part 7 and Part 3 [12,13] respectively), and elastic modulus (according to BS 1881-121 [14]) of the concretes and cement paste are presented in Table 4 . All results were from average of three specimens. Water accessible porosity of concrete was determined by water saturation method. Top and bottom 10 mm of the cylinders were removed and the end surfaces were ground before test. From mass of the saturated surface dry spec- imens determined in air and in water, and mass of the specimens oven dried at 105 /C176C, water accessible porosity of the concretes and cement paste was calculated. Total porosity of the concrete ( Table 4) was calculated from the mixture propor- tion of the concrete ( Table 2 ), total porosity of the LWA ( Table 1 ), and porosity of cement paste determined from mercury intrusion porosimeter. The porosity of ITZ in the concretes was not considered in the calculation. Table 2 Mixture proportions of the concretes. 3.3. Comparison of control normal weight concrete (NC2) and lightweight concretes(LC1–LC4) with similar or lower 28-day strength The control NWAC NC2 had 28-day compressive strength of 49 MPa, which was similar to that of LC1 and LC2, but higher than that of LC3 and LC4. Results, however, show that the LWAC had lower sorptivity and water permeability coefﬁcient and higher resistance to chloride penetration than NC2. This can be attributed mainly to the lower w/c of 0.38 of the LWAC, and consequently lower porosity of cement paste, in comparison to NC2 ( w/ c = 0.54). The results on the resistance to the chloride-ion penetra- tion differed from those from Al-Khaiat and Haque mentioned in the introduction [3]. 3.4. Comparison of the concretes with cement paste of the same w/c Comparing cement paste CP with the concretes LC1 and NC1, it seems that CP had higher sorptivity and lower resistance to chlo- ride-ion penetration reﬂected by the higher charge passed (ASTM C 1202) and chloride diffusion coefﬁcient and surface chloride con- tent (salt ponding test). The high resistance to water and chloride penetration of the concretes was mainly attributed to the increased tortuosity of the pores in concrete due to the presence of aggregates (both LWA and NWA) compared to cement paste. Scanning electron microscopy images of NC1 and LC1 revealed both good and poor quality of the paste at ITZ as compared with the surrounding bulk paste ( Figs. 4 and 5 ). For the LWAC, ITZ with relatively denser paste typically occurred around LWA with undeﬁned por- ous sintered shell from the effect of water absorption and inter- nal curing ( Fig. 5 , top). Therefore, even if this type of LWA is more susceptible to ion penetration, the presence of a well- bonded dense layer of interface paste would serve as an effective barrier. On the other hand, ITZ in LWAC with relatively more porous paste of lower quality typically occurred around LWA with a well-deﬁned dense sintered shell. This type of LWA could serve as an effective barrier to ion penetration on its own due to the presence of the dense outer shell. Despite having a more porous paste layer around the aggregates at these interface zones, the presence of the aggregates (both LWA and NWA) would act as obstacles and increase the path length of ion migration and diffu- sion around the aggregate, instead of going through the aggregate as mentioned before. For the cement paste CP, it was likely that degree of the pore tortuosity is very low without any aggregate inclusion as compared to the concretes NC1 and LC1. This explains why the NWAC and LWAC had lower sorptivity and higher resistance to chloride-ion penetration than the cement paste with similar w/c. The higher resistance of concrete to chloride-ion penetration in comparison to cement paste of the same w/c is consistent with the results by Delegrave [25]. 4. Summary and conclusions Based on the results presented and discussed, following conclu- sions may be drawn: 1. Although the total charge passed, migration coefﬁcient, and dif- fusion coefﬁcient of the LWAC were not signiﬁcantly different from those of NWAC with the same w/c of 0.38, the resistance of the LWAC to chloride-ion penetration decreased with the increase in the cumulative LWA content in the concrete. The water penetration depth under pressure and water sorptivity showed, in general, similar trends. The LWAC with only coarse LWA had similar water sorptivity, water permeability coefﬁ- cient and resi

2.3. Test methods The test methods in this study were adopted from various standards at tropical temperature of 28–30 /C176C. Any other modiﬁcations are separately stated in the text. 2.3.1. Water absorption Initial water absorption and sorptivity of the concretes and cement paste were tested according to ASTM C 1585 [15] by measuring increase in mass of the speci- mens as a function of time with one surface exposed to water. After the test, two parameters were calculated according to Buyle-Bodin and Hadjieva-Zaharieva [16] as follows: /C15 Initial water absorption (kg/m 2) – quantity of water absorbed by a unit surface area during 1st hour of the test. /C15 Sorptivity (kg/m 2 h0.5) – slope of regression curve of the quantity of water absorbed by a unit surface area versus square root of elapsed time from 1 to 24 h. 2.3.2. Water permeability Depth of water penetration was determined according to BS EN 12390-8 [17] with some modiﬁcations. Specimens were roughened on the circumferential sur- face immediately after demolding and cured at 100% RH for 7 days ( Table 3 ). The circumferential surface was then coated with epoxy mortar to ensure one dimen- sional ﬂow of water. Mass of the coated specimens was determined before the per- meability test. The reason for early testing at 7 days was due to a low achievable maximum pressure by the equipment, and thus it would be difﬁcult to obtain water penetration if the specimens were cured longer. The specimens were subjected to a pressure about 0.75 MPa for 14 days. After testing, mass of the specimen was recorded before it was split into two halves (lengthwise) to measure depth of water penetration. Water permeability coefﬁcient was calculated according to Valenta’s equation [18]: KP ¼ d 2 /C1 v 2 /C1 h /C1 t ð2Þ where KP = coefﬁcient of water permeability (m/s); d = water penetration depth (m); v = porosity of concrete; h = water hydraulic head (m); and t = time under pressure (s). Porosity v was calculated from Eq. (3). v ¼ m Adq ð3Þ where m = gain in mass (g); A = cross-sectional area of specimen (mm 2), q = density of water ( /C24 1000 kg/m3). 2.3.3. Resistance of concrete to chloride-ion penetration Resistance of the concrete to chloride-ion penetration was evaluated by the fol- lowing three methods. 2.3.3.1. Rapid chloride penetrability test (RCPT). The test was carried out at 28 days according to ASTM C 1202 using cylindrical specimens ( Table 3 ). Total charges passed after 6 h was obtained from integration of current over the test duration. 2.3.3.2. Rapid migration test. Migration coefﬁcient (or apparent diffusion coefﬁcient) was determined according to NT Build 492 method using specimens as described in Table 3. Each specimen was exposed to a 10% NaCl solution on one side and a 0.3 M NaOH solution on the other. An external potential of 30 V was applied across the specimen for 24 h. After that the specimen was split into two halves (lengthwise). The split surfaces were sprayed with 0.1 N AgNO 3 solution to determine chloride penetration depth, which was then used to calculate migration coefﬁcient accord- ing to the standard. 2.3.3.3. Salt ponding test. Resistance of the selected concretes and the cement paste to chloride-ion penetration was determined according to AASHTO T259 [8] method with some modiﬁcations. One of the modiﬁcations was 3-day water ponding of the specimens prior to salt ponding. Purpose was to create a condition to simulate a dif- fusion process for chloride ions rather

Comparing the LWAC and the control concrete NC1, they had the same w/c for the cement paste, and the difference was mainly the aggregate and characteristics of ITZ between the aggregate and paste matrix. Although LWA particles were porous compared with the NWA, many pores in LWA were discrete and were inaccessible by water (Table 1), especially for those with a denser outer sintered shell. Another factor that affects the sorptivity of the concrete is the size of the pores. In principle, water absorption is caused by capillary effect of pores. The greater the pore size, the smaller the capillary effect. Large pores in LWA may contribute little to water absorption even if they are continuous. Fig. 4 shows two images from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) performed on a polished sample of normal weight concrete NC1. From SEM imaging, it was observed that the typical ITZ around the NWA had more porous paste compared to surrounding bulk paste matrix ( Fig. 4, bottom image) due to the wall effect [21]. The aggregate would act as obstacles and increase the path length of water migration around the aggregate, instead of going through the aggregate. Besides this, dense paste of similar quality as sur- rounding bulk paste matrix was also occasionally observed in cer- tain regions at the ITZ around NWA ( Fig. 4, top image). This would make aggregates effective barriers against water migration into the specimens, and reduce the continuity of capillary pores in the concrete. In the LWAC, denser ITZ between the LWA and cement paste might have reduced sorptivity of the concretes even though they contained porous aggregate particles. Fig. 5 shows two images from SEM performed on a polished sample of LC1. The typical ITZ seen around a coarse LWA with undeﬁned porous sintered shell and undeﬁned interface between the aggregate and paste matrix is shown in the top image. Quality of the paste near the aggregate seems to be similar to that of the bulk paste matrix. In the LWAC, water inside the LWA contributed to internal curing, thus the degree of cement hydration in these concretes would probably be higher compared with that in the control concrete NC1. Examination of the polished concrete samples indicates that LC1 had less unhydrated particles ( Fig. 5 ) than NC1 ( Fig. 4 ). The increased cement hydration would reduce capillary porosity and increase the tortuosity of pores in the cement paste which might have also reduced sorptivity of the LWAC even though the LWA was porous. On the other hand, it has been observed occasionally that there is a different type of ITZ around LWA with denser sintered shell. For such region, the paste is seen to be more porous than the bulk paste matrix, as shown in Fig. 5 (bottom image). The presence of the LWA with a denser shell would have a wall effect similar to that of NC1 ( Fig. 4 , bottom image) as discussed earlier. The dense Fig. 3. Initial water absorption and sorptivity of different mixtures. granite sand Paste paste sand granite ITZ Fig. 4. SEM images on interface transition zone around coarse NWA in NC1 ( Note: D – unhydrated cement particles). X. Liu et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 335–343 339

shell would act as barrier against water migration and increase the path length of water migration around the aggregate, instead of going through the aggregate, thereby reducing the continuity of capillary pores in the concrete. 3.1.2. Water permeability Measured penetration depth of water under pressure and calcu- lated water permeability coefﬁcient of the concretes are summa- rized in Table 5 . The results show that the concretes with w/c of 0.38 had relatively low water permeability (10 /C0 13 m/s) regardless of aggregate type and amount of the porous LWA particles used in the concretes. Comparing the concretes with the same w/c, the LWAC showed higher water permeability than the control concrete NC1. Although there is generally an improved ITZ between LWA and paste matrix and an improved cement hydration due to internal curing by water absorbed in the LWA leading to reduced capillary pores in LWAC, the LWA is porous and water can penetrate through the aggregates under pressure. This means that under pressure the increase in mass ‘‘ m” of the LWAC can be higher than that of the control concrete NC1. The higher mass increase is related to higher porosity ‘‘ v” in the LWAC leading to the higher water permeability coefﬁcient ( Table 5 ). On the other hand, the water penetration depth of the LWAC was lower than that of the control concrete NC1 under pressure ex- cept LC4-30 which will be discussed in next section. This is impor- tant from durability point of view. For example, initiation of corrosion of steel reinforcement embedded in concrete is affected by concentration of chloride ions in the vicinity of the steel bars where the chloride ions can be brought into the concrete by hydraulic pressures. Rate of advancement of the critical chloride front toward the steel is of signiﬁcant importance for consideration of service lifespan of reinforced concrete structures exposed to, e.g. marine environment. 3.1.3. Resistance to chloride-ion penetration Table 6 summarizes resistance of the concretes and correspond- ing cement paste to chloride-ion penetration determined by the various methods described. The LWAC had similar charges passed compared with the control concrete of the same w/c except for LC3 and LC4-30 which showed higher charges passed (LC4-30 will be discussed in next section). The charges passed through the con- cretes, however, were all within the range from 2000 to 4000 cou- lombs which was classiﬁed as ‘‘moderate” chloride penetrability according to ASTM C 1202. In the LWAC, LWA had higher porosity compared with the NWA. However, the porosity in cement paste in the LWAC was probably lower due to the internal curing effects [2,22] compared with that in the NWAC. In addition, the improve- ment in the ITZ between LWA and paste matrix in the LWAC also affected pore structure of the concrete which in turn affected the charges passed. The chloride penetration depth and migration coefﬁcient of the LWAC determined by the NT Build 492 method were similar to or lower than those of the control concrete NC1 of the same w/c.I n this test, the chloride penetration depth was determined by a whit- ish color due to precipitation of AgCl. According to the method, chloride concentration at which the color changes was approxi- mately 0.07 N for ordinary Portland cement concrete. According to Otsuki et al. [23], this whitish color will only be visible when chloride content is >0.15% by mass of cement using 0.1 N AgNO 3 solutions. This means that

Fig. 6 shows proﬁles of chlorides in the selected concretes after the 90-day ponding test. The surface chloride content and chlo- ride diffusion coefﬁcient of the concretes obtained from curve ﬁt- ting of the chloride proﬁles according to Fick’s second law are summarized in Table 6 . Diffusion of chloride ions in NWAC is dependent primarily on pore structure of cement paste and ITZ between the aggregate and paste matrix as the diffusion in the NWA is negligibly small [24]. For concrete with LWA particles, however, chloride ions may diffuse through the porous light- weight aggregate. From the results, although the surface chloride content ( C0) of the LWAC was higher than that of the control con- crete, the increase in the cumulative LWA content generally did not affect the chloride diffusion coefﬁcient of the concrete signif- icantly except for LC4-30 which will be discussed in next section. The higher surface chloride content of LWAC might be attributed to the higher porosities of the LWA. Since diffusion process is re- lated to concentration differentials of chloride in exposure envi- ronment and in concrete pore solution, long-term tests are needed to evaluate diffusion in the LWAC in comparison to NWAC with the same w/c. Comparing the three different tests used for evaluating con- crete resistance to chloride-ion penetration, the chloride diffusion coefﬁcient was calculated based on acid soluble chloride content in the concretes after the 90-day ponding test. Thus the trend established by the diffusion coefﬁcient would be more accurate than that by the migration coefﬁcient and charge passed. How- ever, it should be mentioned that the chloride contents in the salt ponding test were determined from small sampling mass (5 g) of the dried concrete slabs. Thus, relative proportion of cement paste and aggregate might vary from sample to sample, possibly lead- ing to random errors in the results. 3.2. Effect of cumulative content of lightweight aggregate particles in concrete and crushed ﬁne LWA Although the total charge passed, migration coefﬁcient, and diffusion coefﬁcient of the LWAC were not signiﬁcantly different from those of NC1 with the same w/c of 0.38, it was observed from Table 6 that resistance of the LWAC to chloride penetration decreased with the increase in the cumulative spherical LWA con- tent in the concrete (LC1–LC3). The water penetration depth un- der pressure ( Table 5 ) and water sorptivity showed, in general, similar trends. As for the all-LWAC LC4, the actual sorptivity, water penetration depth under pressure, and resistance to chloride-ion penetration for an effective w/c of 0.38 would probably lie in the range between the results of LC4-25 and LC4-30, explained earlier under Section 2.2. Porosity, pore size distribution, and pore tortuosity of concrete have signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the resistance of concrete to penetra- tion of harmful substances through different mechanisms. For LC1–LC3, they had similar paste matrix (characteristics and vol- ume), but increased cumulative volumes of LWA and decreased volume of NWA. The total porosity was increased from about 28.2% in LC1 to 33.5% in LC3 ( Table 4 ) due to the increase in the cumulative content of LWA. More than 30% of the total porosity in the LWAC can be detected by vacuum saturation method as water accessible porosity. For mortars with normal weight sand, Delagrave et al. [25] found that the transport coefﬁcient of chloride ions decreased with an increase in sa
